questions.question: Which of the following browsers can you use to access Notion Calendar for web?

options.text: Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge, and Arc
options.explanation: All listed browsers are explicitly supported for Notion Calendar on the web.

options.text: Internet Explorer, Opera, Vivaldi, and Brave
options.explanation: These browsers are not mentioned in the supported list; Internet Explorer is especially unsupported.

options.text: Only Chrome and Safari
options.explanation: While Chrome and Safari are supported, Firefox, Edge, and Arc are also supported.

options.text: Only Edge and Firefox
options.explanation: Edge and Firefox are supported, but Chrome, Safari, and Arc are also supported.

questions.question: On which operating systems can you run Notion Calendar for web?

options.text: macOS, Windows, ChromeOS, and Linux
options.explanation: The article states that Notion Calendar for web works on any OS, specifically naming those four.

options.text: Only macOS and Windows
options.explanation: ChromeOS and Linux are also supported, so this answer is incomplete.

options.text: iOS and Android
options.explanation: Those are mobile platforms; the question asks about the web version, which runs on desktop OSes.

options.text: Only Linux
options.explanation: Linux is supported, but it is not the only OS; the others are also supported.

questions.question: Which method can you use to install Notion Calendar on macOS via a package manager?

options.text: Homebrew Cask
options.explanation: The article mentions a Homebrew Cask is available for installing Notion Calendar on macOS.

options.text: MacPorts
options.explanation: MacPorts is not referenced in the article as a way to install Notion Calendar.

options.text: npm install notion-calendar
options.explanation: npm is a Node.js package manager and is unrelated to the native macOS app distribution.

options.text: Direct download from the Windows store
options.explanation: The Windows Store provides Windows apps; macOS users need the macOS download or Homebrew Cask.

questions.question: What is the minimum macOS version required for Notion Calendar to run?

options.text: macOS 11 (Big Sur) and above
options.explanation: The article explicitly notes that Notion Calendar supports macOS 11 and later.

options.text: macOS 10.12 (Sierra)
options.explanation: Sierra is older than the required macOS 11; it is not supported.

options.text: macOS 10.15 (Catalina)
options.explanation: Catalina is earlier than macOS 11, and the article specifies 11 as the minimum.

options.text: Any macOS version, as long as you have Homebrew installed
options.explanation: Even with Homebrew, the app still requires macOS 11 or newer.

questions.question: Which mobile platforms provide a Notion Calendar home screen widget?

options.text: iOS 14+ (iPhone & iPad) and Android 8+
options.explanation: The article states that the home screen widget is available on iOS 14+ for iPhone/iPad and Android 8+.

options.text: Only iOS 15+ devices
options.explanation: Android also supports the widget, and iOS 14+ is sufficient, not just 15+.

options.text: Only Android 10+ devices
options.explanation: iOS devices also have the widget, and Android 8+ is the minimum, not 10+.

options.text: iOS 13 and Android 7
options.explanation: The minimum versions are iOS 14 and Android 8; earlier versions are not supported.

questions.question: If a user wants to hide event titles on their lock screen widget for privacy, which setting should they adjust?

options.text: Turn off "Event titles" in the lock screen widget settings
options.explanation: The article notes that you can turn off event titles in the lock screen widget for privacy.

options.text: Enable "Do Not Disturb" mode on the device
options.explanation: Do Not Disturb silences notifications but does not hide titles on the widget.

options.text: Switch the widget to week view
options.explanation: The mobile widget does not support a week view; this setting does not affect title visibility.

options.text: Delete the widget from the home screen
options.explanation: Deleting the widget removes it entirely; the question asks about hiding titles while keeping the widget.

questions.question: Which of the following actions is NOT possible on Notion Calendar for mobile?

options.text: Create a new event with an auto‑generated video‑conference link
options.explanation: The mobile app does not support auto‑generated conferencing links; they must be added manually.

options.text: Edit an event that you created on iOS and are the organizer of
options.explanation: Editing events you created on iOS as the organizer is allowed.

options.text: Customize how many days are displayed in a home screen widget
options.explanation: Widget settings let you choose the number of days shown.

options.text: Exclude a specific calendar from appearing in the lock screen widget
options.explanation: You can exclude calendars from both home and lock screen widgets.

questions.question: Why can't a user set Notion Calendar as the default calendar app on mobile devices?

options.text: Operating‑system restrictions prevent third‑party apps from becoming the system default for calendar data
options.explanation: Both iOS and Android limit which apps can be set as the default calendar; Notion Calendar is not permitted to replace the native calendar app.

options.text: Notion Calendar does not support syncing with other calendars on mobile
options.explanation: The app does sync across platforms; the limitation is about default‑app status, not syncing.

options.text: The app requires a paid subscription to become the default
options.explanation: There is no mention of a subscription requirement for default status.

options.text: Users must first install the desktop version before they can set it as default on mobile
options.explanation: Desktop installation has no effect on mobile default‑app capabilities.

questions.question: A user reports that they can only view a one‑day schedule on their Android phone. Which limitation described in the article explains this behavior?

options.text: Mobile view is limited to one, two, or three‑day views
options.explanation: The article states that on mobile you are limited to a one‑, two‑, or three‑day view.

options.text: The Android app does not support any custom view settings
options.explanation: While view options are limited, the cause is the specific day‑range limitation, not a lack of any custom settings.

options.text: The user needs to enable week view in widget settings
options.explanation: Week view is not available on mobile at all, so this would not resolve the issue.

options.text: The app requires a premium subscription to unlock multi‑day views
options.explanation: The limitation is built into the mobile app regardless of subscription level.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes how Notion Calendar ensures data consistency across its web, desktop, and mobile versions?

options.text: All platforms sync seamlessly, so changes made on one device appear on the others
options.explanation: The article emphasizes that the mobile experience syncs seamlessly with web and desktop, implying consistent data across all platforms.

options.text: Only the web version stores events; desktop and mobile are read‑only views
options.explanation: Desktop and mobile can create and edit events (with some limitations), so they are not read‑only.

options.text: Each platform maintains its own separate calendar database
options.explanation: Separate databases would prevent the seamless syncing described in the article.

options.text: Data is synced only when the user manually presses a "Sync" button
options.explanation: The article does not mention a manual sync; syncing happens automatically.
